html:
Grid and Parallel Lines
Here, you see a grid of lines on a ground plane: some run left-to-right, others front-to-back.
⚠ Figure failed to load

plan.contextChunk:
also have the same horizon line. In @fig-palma_horizon, the lines in
the building facade that are parallel to the ground also converge to a
vanishing point that lies on the horizon line. In this particular
examples, the lines in the facade also happen to be parallel to one of
the axis of the floor tiles, converging to the same vanishing point on
the right side of the figure.

The ground plane is a special plane because it supports most objects.
This is different than a wall. Both are planes, but the ground has an
ecological importance that a wall does not have. The point of contact
between objects and the ground plane informs about its distance. The
distance between the contact point and the horizon line is related to
distance between the object and the camera. Objects further away have a
contact point projecting onto a higher point in the image plane.

@fig-horizon_line_cross_products illustrates how to detect the horizon
line using homogeneous coordinates. If lines $\boldsymbol{l}_1$ and
$\boldsymbol{l}_2$, in homogeneous coordinates, correspond to the 2D
projection of 3D parallel lines, we can obtain the vanishing point as
their intersection (cross-product,
$\boldsymbol{l}_1 \times \boldsymbol{l}_2$). If we now have another set
of parallel lines, contained in the same plane, $\boldsymbol{l}_3$ and
$\boldsymbol{l}_4$, we can get another vanishing point by computing
their cross-product. Finally, the horizon line is the line that passes
through both points that can be computed as the cross-product of the
vanishing points in homogeneous coordinates.

![Using parallel lines, in homogeneous coordinates, to compute vanishing points and the horizon line.](figures/imaging_geometry/horizon_line.png){width="90%" #fig-horizon_line_cross_products}

### Detecting Vanishing Points

@fig-vanishing_lines shows the office scene and three vanishing points
computed manually. In this scene there are three dominant orientations
along orthogonal directions. The three vanishing points are estimated by
selecting pairs of long edges in the image corresponding to parallel 3D
lines in the scene and finding their intersection.

![Most object boundaries in this photograph are oriented parallel to one of the world-coordinates reference frame (the world-coordinates frame is chosen in this way on purpose). All the parallel 3D lines converge on the image plane in a vanishing point. In this picture there are three main vanishing points, each of them related to one of the world-coordinates frame.](figures/single_view_3d/office_vanishing_points_3.png){width="100%" #fig-vanishing_lines}

Vanishing points can be extracted automatically from images by first
detecting image edges and their orientations (like we did in chapter
@sec-simplesystem) and then using RANSAC (@sec-RANSAC) to group the edges: every pair of edges will
vote for a vanishing point and the point with the largest support will

elements: tan ground plane grid (with both thick and thin black lines labeled l1, l2, l3, l4)
vanishing point from l1 and l2 (green dot, labeled l1 × l2)
vanishing point from l3 and l4 (green dot, labeled l3 × l4)
intersection points/vanishing points labeled with cross-products
horizon line label with formula h = (l1 × l2) × (l3 × l4)

interactions.label: Move vanishing points
interactions.teaches: Shows how changing the orientation of sets of parallel lines in the plane makes their vanishing points move along the horizon line.

interactions.label: Rotate ground grid
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates how rotating the ground plane alters the directions of grid lines and redistributes their vanishing points.

interactions.label: Show/hide math labels
interactions.teaches: Allows focus on geometry alone or combined with symbolic homogeneous coordinate expressions.

demo_steps.title: Grid and Parallel Lines
demo_steps.narration: Here, you see a grid of lines on a ground plane: some run left-to-right, others front-to-back. Notice that each set stays parallel within the plane. These lines are labeled l1 to l4 for clarity.

demo_steps.title: Finding Vanishing Points
demo_steps.narration: Let's highlight how parallel lines meet in the image. See how the pairs (l1, l2) and (l3, l4) each converge toward a separate vanishing point marked by green dots. These intersections are not random—they are determined by the direction of the lines in 3D.

demo_steps.title: Horizon by Vanishing Points
demo_steps.narration: Now, as we rotate the grid, both sets of vanishing points slide along the red horizon line. The horizon is special: it contains all vanishing points of lines embedded in the ground plane. The red line, computed as h = (l1 × l2) × (l3 × l4), reflects the true geometry of the ground.

demo_steps.title: Focus On Geometry
demo_steps.narration: You can also hide the mathematical labels to focus entirely on the geometric relationships: how all the essential points and lines depend on the directions of lines and the grid’s orientation. This helps you see the structure with or without the symbolic overlay.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Slightly elevated, looking straight ahead at the plane with horizon line aligned at upper third, showing a wide field to encompass both vanishing points.
interactionPlan.notes: Use subtle ground grid coloring for depth. Vanishing points must remain on the horizon as controls are animated. Intersection math and dynamic label placement are important for clarity; ensure thick/thin lines are distinguishable. Use font and color to make formula overlays stand out but not obscure geometry.

attempts.html:
Grid and Parallel Lines
Here, you see a grid of lines on a ground plane: some run left-to-right, others front-to-back.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The color and thickness of the grid lines, vanishing points, ground and sky match faithfully, but a minor difference is the slightly heavier outline thickness in the original.
The labels l1-l4 are well-placed on the bold lines but are slightly more cramped and smaller compared to the source figure.
In the generated figure, interactivity elements and step annotations are present that are not in the original static figure.
Narration panel and step-by-step buttons appear in the interactive figure, slightly changing the overall composition.
There is a faint depth grid (brown lines) in the generated version that is not strongly pronounced in the source.
evaluation.notes: Excellent reproduction with a faithful interactive, pedagogical extension; only minor differences in label size, line thickness, and extra UI/annotation content.
action_items: Increase the font size and spacing of the l1-l4 labels for enhanced readability and better correspondence with the source.
Slightly increase the boldness or thickness of the main black grid lines to match the visual impact of the original.
Optionally, adjust the palette or fade the faint brown grid lines to more closely match their subtlety in the source.

feedback.rationale: All critical scores are high, no failure modes are noted, and only minor visual refinements are suggested—core concept and execution are correct and faithful.
action_items: Increase the font size and spacing of the l1-l4 labels for enhanced readability and better correspondence with the source.

Optionally, adjust the palette or fade the faint brown grid lines to more closely match their subtlety in the source.
